sentences.text: I am Dina Wolfe, ne Denderowicz, born in Warsaw, Poland, April 20, 1937.
My mom and dad, Isaac and Hinda Denderowicz; my grandmother, Chaya [?
Rahel ?] Denderowicz; and my Uncle Saul, known as Uncle [INAUDIBLE].
And these are the people that lived in our apartment together.
He was born March 2nd, 1910.
She was born June 15, 1914.
They're fragments.
There is no continuation, but fragments of that I remember.
My parents, of course, told me where we lived, which was 23 [POLISH].
Later on, I understand that was part of the Warsaw ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I remember my grandmother.
She was a-- a little lady.
And when I was born, she said, oh, another girl.
Because her other two sons, who were married already at the time, had girls.
Right.
My father was the second youngest of five sons.
Now, the other two sons perished in the war with the children.
My Uncle Sam, my father's-- that was the middle one, that my father's elder brother-- came to Canada in 1934.
My Uncle Saul-- --and my father and mother and I, but-- were survived in Russia.
And my grandmother, I was told, perished in the war-- after the war, actually, or three months before the war ended by some people, not the Nazis.
She was killed.
The stories came back that some Poles killed her after the war.
Some said it was three months before the war.
It's very difficult to know at that time who killed who at what time.
The end result is, of course, that she did not survive the war.
I remember there was a-- there was a love, you know?
It's a feeling-- not a memory, but a feeling of a memory.
We lived in an apartment and we had-- what I do remember is a very long kitchen.
At the end of that length was a window.
On the right hand side of that room where two cots, one after the other.
The first cot was where my grandmother slept.
The one and the head of that was where my Uncle Saul slept.
And I used to run in the morning and cuddle with her.
And then I would run into my uncle's bed.
And till this day, we joke about it.
I take my shirt-- As a matter of fact, we have it on film.
On his 80th birthday, I took my shirt and tied up to his because that's what I used to do, tie up my nightgown to his so he wouldn't leave me.
So my-- my uncle and my grandmother were a very big part of my life.
My father was busy working, mother did not.
My father is a tailor and he had, in those days I guess, that was the norm to have a factory inside your own apartment.
So he had a factory and he had people working in the apartment.
It was I guess after I was born that things became a little easier financially for them and he was becoming more and more successful.
And he was quite successful towards the end, just before the war.
Well, I remember there were always a lot of people coming around.
My parents were very active in the drama group and choir, and they always tell me that they performed in the [INAUDIBLE] Theater.
So they were quite an artistic bunch that used to come up.
And I was the only child of that whole group so there was a lot of attention lavished on me and they taught me a lot of things.
And I guess one of them was performing for them.
After the war, one of them found us and he was--
--he was so excited.
All he could keep saying was, Dina, it is you; Dina, it is you; Dina, it is you.
And I was like nine years old?
And he couldn't believe that that little girl of two was now nine and survived the war.
Did they teach you songs?
Oh, yes.
Yes.
There was one that I remember, but it's in Polish.
And I don't remember the whole thing, but it used to go something like [SINGING IN POLISH] That's the one I remember.
[LAUGHING] The years before the war-- --are very vague, as I said, just fragments.
Because when the war broke out, I was two.
Yes.
What I do remember is parks with my mom.
Mom would take me to the park and she'd feed me all sorts of stuff and we'd go and see the geese in the [INAUDIBLE] in Warsaw, Poland.
So-- and I-- and I guess over the years, you know, from that time on, she and I were very, very close.
Sometimes she used to, you know, in my later years, I guess, she was a little difficult, but always-- always bright, always forthright, and a lot of the times demanding.
Well, with the first bombing of Warsaw.
That would have been September 1, 1939.
All the people, and we lived in a very Jewish concentrated area of Warsaw-- --the sirens came about, everybody went outside and stood in the gates.
The buildings were gated-- a courtyard gated.
And inside that courtyard, there were all sorts of apartment buildings.
The bombing started and I do remember-- I don't remember the danger of it, but I do remember then comfort of it and being in my mother's arms.
And the bombs started to fall and the buildings started to collapse.
And the police would not let people out of the gates because there were fires, there were sirens, there were horses, there were fire brigades.
Those were the days of the horse and buggy.
So with the pressure, I suppose, people, people just broke out of the gates and started running.
And Mother carrying me ran a whole day and into the night.
And it was quite late at night with running.
She-- I don't know how she encountered one of her friends, who rescued us and took us into a basement.
That was the very first day of bombing of Warsaw, September 1.
It was within Warsaw.
You-- masses were running.
People were running in one direction and encountered other people running towards them who stopped them and said, don't run any further because such and such a street is burning.
So you make another turn and other people keep-- come running saying, don't go any further, we were just there, this street is burning.
It was chaotic.
It must have been something incredible.
I-- it's like a dream.
I cannot tell you exactly.
I wish Mom were here.
She would describe it very, very eloquently.
The next morning, everybody congregated in their own homes to see who, what how survived.
Because in this rush and panic, my father, my uncle, my grandmother, and mother all dispersed in different directions.
So in the morning, my mother tried to make her way.
And it wasn't an easy thing because there were barricades.
Streets were crowded with dead horses and wagons overturned and cars and people, mostly people-- dead people.

My father and my uncle got back to our house before, and since they didn't see Mother, the two of them ventured out into the streets turning over every dead woman and child to see if it was my mother and I.
I'm sure it must have been great joy for them to encounter one another again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had no home.
I don't know what span of time, you know, whether it was the same day, whether it was a few days later, whether they were able to collect some of their belongings if anything was left.
But what I do remember is that we went outside of Warsaw to a village called [POLISH] near the Russian border, where my mother and I used to vacation summertimes and where her family lived.
She had cousins and they lived there all year round.
And we used to come there to refresh in the summertime.
So we went there-- this was still, from what we understood, standing still and was not bombed as yet.
Who knew what was going on?
I don't imagine my mom and dad even suspected what would happen.
So Mom-- grandmother, rather-- didn't want to go and neither did my Uncle Saul.
So the two of them stayed in Warsaw.
But my father and mother and I, we went on to the family.
[PUASES] We were there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] quite a few months.
I'm not quite sure what length of time, during which time my mother was deathly ill.
She was hospitalized.
I was very ill.
My mother had diphtheria, and then I got that.
And she, she came out of the hospital and with diphtheria, you really have to watch your diet, I think.
And her cousin was making fresh chicken livers with onions.
And although the cousin didn't want to give it to her is the story that I got, Mom had very good persuasive powers-- and she had some.
And that night, they had to call the doctor because-- and the doctor said, either she's going to die or she's going to make it.
You know, there's nothing to do.
Thankfully, Mom had a good constitution and she made it through.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later on, when the Russians came, everybody left, everybody was evacuated out of there.
No.
The scarlet fever was later on, when we left Siberia en route to [RUSSIAN].
That's from-- from this [RUSSIAN], they took us to Archangel.
Well, that-- you know, it becomes a little clearer the picture because then I was already-- I don't now, maybe three and a half, four, you now.
So we went to Archangel.
There were-- cars were provided, cattle cars were provided.
And all the people on the Russian border, Russia-Poland were evacuated to a safety area.
By the Russian government.
And after three or four weeks in those, we wound up on I think it must have been the White Sea because Archangel is on the White Sea.
What I understand was that Russia and Poland at that time did not enjoy very friendly relations.
Polish government was exiled to London, England at the onset of the war.

And it wasn't until the treaty-- I think it was in 1941 but I'm not quite sure of my history dates-- that the Russian and Polish governments had a treaty that the Polish citizens residing in Russia from the time that they were evacuated from the time of the war in 1939 were allowed to leave that area and settle in other cities in Russia, either the capital provincial cities.
So we were in Archangel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 14 or 17 months during which time my father-- and we were as prisoners there because you couldn't go, you couldn't leave the place.
First of all, where it was and secondly, if you left, you had to have transportation.
You couldn't-- either you went into the ocean or you went into the forest.
Neither one of them gave you a very good choice because if you didn't have transportation, you were lost.
No, I don't, especially not from the Jewish community that was there.
Originally there was a prison, and it was a Russian prison of many years in existence.
And we were sort of the addition to that-- to that area.
I couldn't tell you, but there must have been at least 10, 14 Villages.
And they were called a [INAUDIBLE].
And each [INAUDIBLE] had a number, rather than a name.
[INAUDIBLE] number one, [INAUDIBLE] number two.
After the war, we met a lot of people who were [INAUDIBLE] number six, [INAUDIBLE] 23, [INAUDIBLE] 14, you know.
Number one.
That's where my mother-- there was a hospital there.
My mother worked in the hospital.
My father worked with the horses.
He was grazing the horses at night.
You should Speak to dad about his experiences with the bears and the wolves, how they came around and how he was responsible for the horses.
Sometimes he had to deliver things from one [INAUDIBLE] to another.
And the frost was like 56 below, centigrade.
We're not talking like-- pretty cold.
Paper to wrap around your feet, things to keep you warm.
And Mother worked in the hospital.
She was a nurse with no nursing experience.
She was a nurse.
And when my father was working at night raising the horses, then I would go with her to the hospital and stay there because it was very close to the house.
In those days, my mother was the frightened one.
My father was the brave one.
So I at the age of four used to hold her hand and take her to the hospital.
In the [INAUDIBLE]?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I remember having an infection.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In one of those trips with my mother, I somehow infected my right thumb.
And--
--there was blood poisoning setting in.
And my father ran to get the doctor, the doctor wasn't there.
So they got-- so they got a vet, OK, with a pair of pliers-- that I remember so vividly-- tore off the nail.
And that probably saved my life.
And sitting on my father's lap while this was done.
I had very devoted parents, I mean they really-- they really showed that emotions towards me, you know?
I used to be envied by my friends.
They always knew-- they always knew-- that my mom and dad loved me a great deal.
It was a good feeling.
It's a good feeling.
At that-- in-- in Archangel, I do not remember having friends or-- and I don't even know if there were other people with children.
Life was very segregated.
You had your little house, which consisted of a room-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --your-- your immediate family.
Things were fairly secretive.
I do not recall having a social life, not just for myself but for my parents as well.
None at all.
None at all.
Nothing at all.
This was not-- this was not a place where people lived a normal life.
I don't know what went on, like in the prison where the-- the Russian prisoners were.
But on that [INAUDIBLE] itself, I don't recall ever having books or radio, newspapers, some sort of communication even with other people.
I don't recall.
But I do recall that-- just that hospital and where it stood.
It's-- it's a vision that stands in front of my eyes, the forest where it was in proportion to our house.
And the life that went on there, food.
A lot of mushrooms.
[LAUGHING] About A lot of mushrooms.
Mother learned how to-- how to pick the mushrooms because there were many incidences of poison mushrooms and fruits.
Berries, there were a lot of berries.
And during the winter months, it was lean pickings.
In those days, yes.
After that, I-- I-- don't remember hunger to the point of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suffering, prolonged suffering.
I don't.

I do know that my father when we first landed in the [INAUDIBLE], in that area, being not citizens of that area or just being guests of cousins and when we were taken away, we had nothing to take with us.
But the people who lived there, they took whatever products they had to take with them.
And when the train stopped, they took some wood and stone and started to cook a little bit this, that, and the other.
And my father went around with a cup begging a spoonful from each, whoever cooked for his child.
They didn't have anything.
I'm sure Mom and Dad were very hungry, but they did provide for me.
Right.
No.
Russian was a foreign language, as was Yiddish.
It as a foreign language.
All those languages came later.
I learned Russian there in Archangel, I started.
I guess there must have been some contact with Russian people, whether it was people who came into the house or whether it was some contact.
I don't recall.
But when we left Archangel and eventually settled in [RUSSIAN], I attended school.
Well, whatever reason was what we left, I think it was when the Polish government did have that treaty with the Russians that they let the Polish citizens go.
And again, the Russian government provided cattle cars and all the people left.
And after being in Archangel in Siberia in this cold, cold, cold, frozen world, I think that [INAUDIBLE] was headed for Asia-- Tashkent, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan.
And most people reached those places because there were a lot of Jews there afterwards.
Unfortunately, I got ill en route.
What happened?
I had scarlet fever.
And we had to leave the [INAUDIBLE] in Ulyanovsk.
Ulyanovsk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we left there, and my father found, by some fluke, a doctor who took me into a hospital, saved my life.
My mother stayed with me, father left to try and find some work, to try and find some accommodations.
People were dropping left, right, and center dying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I got out of the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] MY father returned from where he found a job.
He came back.
From what he tells me, he did not recognize Mother.
In the two-week or three-week span, she had become totally emaciated.
She sold her clothing, she sold her coat to get orange to bring me to the hospital.
She ate the peel, I ate the orange.
So when my father returned, she was quite a sight.
And then they took us-- my father took us to where he had found a job, which as I said, it was [INAUDIBLE].
That's right.
I think I was in the hospital for three weeks, possibly a little longer.
It took a little while till I got into a hospital.
There were children, there were so many children.
As the [INAUDIBLE] were passing Ulyansk, they-- children were taken off, the ones who were sick.
There was a lot of whooping cough that I remember.
Being taken-- children were taken out to the balcony or in a building that had no roof or had no walls, you know.
I was lucky.
I was just one of the lucky ones that my father did recognize the doctor and--
From Poland, yes.
He recognized him on a street walking behind him.
He ran after him.
He kept calling, doctor, doctor.
And the man turned around.
He said, how did you recognize that I was a doctor?
And my father told him by his clothing.
Being a tailor paid off.
And he told him that he has a little girl who is-- who is very ill and could he help?
And just by fluke at that time, that particular Polish doctor was opening up a clinic for infectious diseases for children.
And he came to the hospital with a wagon to get me and my mother had to keep the nurses busy while they stole me out of the hospital and put me into the clinic.
And that was my saving grace.
So I did get better.
And then my father came.
Oh, terrific.
